headline: Samsung and SK Hynix tumble 9%+ as chip rout spreads from Wall Street
detail: This is the headline that matters most for your book. A 9%+ single-day drop in the world's two biggest memory chipmakers signals the semi selloff is broadening globally, not just a one-name story. Your leveraged semi exposure - SOXL, SMH, and indirectly QQQ - all feel this directly.
rec: Seriously consider trimming SOXL here or at minimum confirming your $200 stop is live. No scanner long setup supports adding chip exposure into this kind of sector-wide selling.

headline: Apple plans five new iPhones through 2027, eyes Chinese-made chips
detail: Apple exploring Chinese-made chips is a long-term headwind for US chip suppliers like TSMC and Qualcomm, though the 2027 timeline makes this a slow burn. Near-term it adds uncertainty to the chip demand narrative at a bad moment. No direct holding impact today but it adds a negative layer to the semi thesis.
rec: Nothing to trade today on this - it's a multi-year story. But if you're considering adding chip names, this is another reason to wait for the dust to settle rather than buying this dip.

headline: OpenAI considers handing 5% stake to US government
detail: If true, this deepens OpenAI's political ties and could accelerate US government AI spending - broadly bullish for the AI infrastructure names (data centers, chips, networking). Your CRWD exposure benefits indirectly from continued enterprise AI/security spending momentum.
rec: CRWD is already up 7.4% for you and holding well - no action needed today, but this headline reinforces the AI security spend story. Hold and let your stop at $590 do the work.

headline: Google loses $4.7B EU antitrust battle
detail: This confirms Europe's willingness to levy massive fines on big US tech, adding regulatory overhang to mega-cap names. QQQ has heavy Google/Alphabet weighting so this is a mild drag. Not a crisis, but another headwind layered on top of today's chip selloff.
rec: No action warranted - QQQ is up 1.47% for you with a comfortable stop at $670. This headline doesn't change the trade, just keeps the regulatory risk narrative alive in the background.
